blocks.text: JavaScript can do math the same way a calculator can. You already know most of the symbols plus, minus, multiply, divide. There are five operators total, and each one has a job.

blocks.alt: Five cards showing the five math operators: + for add, - for subtract, * for multiply, / for divide, and % for remainder, each with a code example
blocks.caption: These five operators are the building blocks of all math in JavaScript.

blocks.label: One difference from math class
blocks.text: Multiplication uses * (not ×) and division uses / (not ÷). Your keyboard doesn't have those symbols so JavaScript uses these instead.

A number on its own is fine for one calculation. But what if you need to use that number again later? Or change it? That's where variables come in. You store your numbers in variables, then use the variable names in your math. JavaScript swaps in the value automatically.

blocks.alt: Four step flowchart showing: declare variables with let, store numbers in them, use the variable names in a math expression, then log the result to the console
blocks.caption: You write the variable name JavaScript looks up the number stored inside it and uses that.

blocks.desc: JavaScript reads price (20) and tax (2), adds them, and stores 22 into total. You never have to type the actual numbers again just the names.

blocks.label: Why this matters
blocks.text: If you change price from 20 to 35, total automatically becomes 37 the next time it's calculated. You changed one variable and everything that depends on it updates. That's the whole point of variables.

The % operator is the one that looks strangest at first. It doesn't give you the result of dividing it gives you the leftover after dividing as evenly as possible. That leftover is called the remainder.

blocks.alt: Ten numbered dots grouped into three boxes of three, with one dot left over in a dashed box labeled 'left over'. The result is 1.
blocks.caption: 10 % 3 = 1 because 3 fits into 10 three full times, with 1 left over.

blocks.label: The even/odd trick
blocks.text: Any number % 2 gives you either 0 or 1. If the answer is 0, the number is even. If it's 1, the number is odd. Game developers use this constantly for example, to do something every other turn.

Updating a variable using itself is so common adding to a score, losing a life, collecting coins that JavaScript has shortcuts for it. Instead of writing the full thing every time, you can use a shorter version.

blocks.alt: Side by side table: the long way to write score = score + 10 versus the shortcut score += 10, repeated for -=, *=, ++, and --
blocks.caption: The shortcut does exactly the same thing it just saves you from typing the variable name twice.

blocks.desc: The most common shortcut of all adds exactly 1. You'll see this in almost every program that counts something.

blocks.text: JavaScript follows the same math rules you learned in school. Multiplication and division happen before addition and subtraction. If you want a different order, use parentheses they always run first.

blocks.alt: Two expressions side by side: 2 + 3 * 4 equals 14 because * runs first, versus (2 + 3) * 4 equals 20 because parentheses run first
blocks.caption: Same numbers, different answer all because of where the parentheses go.

blocks.label: When in doubt, use parentheses
blocks.text: If you're not sure what order JavaScript will evaluate your math, add parentheses to make it explicit. There's no penalty for using them and they make your intention obvious to anyone reading your code.
Here's a code block that uses everything from this lesson at once. Read through each line before running it try to predict what the final value of score will be.

lines: // Is the score even or odd?

blocks.label: Predict before you run
blocks.text: Before hitting Run, write down what you think score will be at the end. Then run it and compare. If you were wrong, trace through each line again this is the most important skill in programming.

exercises.title: Run the math
tasks: The code is already written just hit Run.
Look at each line printed in the console.
Which result surprised you? Why do you think it came out that way?
Change one of the numbers and run it again.
exercises.hint: Just hit Run first. Read the output. Then start changing numbers.

exercises.title: Your first variable math
tasks: The code is already written just hit Run.
You should see 5 printed in the console.
Read each line and make sure you understand why saving is 5.
Change money to 20 and run again. What does saving become?
exercises.hint: saving = money - 5. So if money is 10, saving is 10 - 5 = 5.

checks.label: something is printed to the console

exercises.title: Use one variable inside another
tasks: brotherAge is already set to 5.
Create a new variable called sisterAge and set it to 10 + brotherAge.
On the next line, write: console.log(sisterAge)
Run it you should see 15 printed.
Change brotherAge to 8 and run again. Does sisterAge update?
exercises.hint: let sisterAge = 10 + brotherAge; use the variable name, not the number.

exercises.title: Read the recipe
tasks: Two variables are declared and a third one called total is already calculated.
Your only job: add console.log(total) at the bottom and hit Run.
You should see 8 printed.
Now change apples to 10. What does total become without touching the total line?
exercises.hint: Just add console.log(total); at the very bottom one line is all you need.

exercises.title: Fill in the math
tasks: Two variables are declared: length and width.
Your job: complete the area line by writing length * width after the = sign.
Run it you should see 40 printed.
Change length to 8 and run again. Does area update automatically?
exercises.hint: let area = length * width; use the variable names, not the actual numbers.

exercises.title: Change one thing, watch everything update
tasks: A price calculator is already set up. Hit Run and read the output.
Now change price to 25 and quantity to 2. Don't touch the total line.
Run it again total should update on its own.
Try a few more combinations. Notice that you never have to rewrite the math.
exercises.hint: Only change the numbers on lines 1 and 2. The total line calculates itself.

checks.name: total
checks.label: total equals 50 when price is 25 and quantity is 2

exercises.title: Build it from scratch
tasks: Declare a variable called age and set it to your age.
Declare a variable called yearsLeft and set it to 100 - age.
Declare a variable called doubleAge and set it to age * 2.
Log all three with labels.
Bonus: log a sentence using all three like 'I am 13, double that is 26, and I have 87 years until 100.'
exercises.hint: Use variable names in your math, not raw numbers: let yearsLeft = 100 - age;

exercises.title: Predict the remainder
tasks: Before running: write down your prediction for each % expression below.
Then run it and check which ones you got right.
Can you figure out the pattern for what % 2 always gives you?
exercises.hint: Think about how many times the second number fits into the first then what's left over.

checks.label: at least one remainder of 1 was logged

exercises.title: Use the shortcuts
tasks: A variable called lives is set to 3.
Use lives-- to lose one life. Log lives.
A variable called coins is set to 0.
Use coins += 10 three times. Log coins after each.
Then use coins *= 2 to double them. Log the final result.
exercises.hint: lives-- subtracts 1. coins += 10 adds 10. Both are shortcuts you don't need the full version.

exercises.title: Predict the order
tasks: Before running: write down the result of each expression on paper.
Then run the code and check.
For any wrong predictions: trace through the order of operations step by step.
exercises.hint: * and / always run before + and - unless you use parentheses.

exercises.title: Score calculator
tasks: Start with score = 0.
Use += to add 50 points.
Use *= to triple it.
Use -= to subtract 25.
Use ++ to add 1 more.
Log 'Final score: ' + score. The answer should be 126.
Then log whether the score is even or odd using %.
exercises.hint: Do each step one at a time and log after each to track your progress.

exercises.title: Build a math story
tasks: You start with 100 coins. Write each step as code:
You spend 30 coins at the shop.
You find a chest that doubles your coins.
You collect 15 more coins.
You split your coins equally with a friend (divide by 2).
Log 'You have X coins left.' at the end.
Bonus: log whether your final coin count is even or odd.
exercises.hint: Use -=, *=, += and /= for each step. Track the value by logging after each operation.

challenges.title: The answer should be 20 not 14
challenges.description: This expression should calculate (2 + 3) * 4 = 20, but it's giving 14. One character fix will do it.
challenges.hint: Think about what parentheses do to order of operations.
challenges.explanation: Without parentheses, * runs before +, so 3 * 4 = 12, then 2 + 12 = 14. To get 20, add parentheses: (2 + 3) * 4.

challenges.title: The score isn't doubling correctly
challenges.description: score should be doubled, but something is wrong. After all steps, score should be 200.
challenges.hint: Look carefully at line 2. Does it actually change score?
challenges.explanation: score + 50 calculates the result but throws it away it never stores it back into score. Fix: change it to score += 50 so score actually gets updated to 100 before being doubled to 200.

challenges.title: Is the player's turn even or odd?
challenges.description: This code should log 'Even turn' when turnNumber is even and 'Odd turn' when it's odd. But it always says the same thing. Find the bug.
challenges.hint: To check if a number is even, what should you divide by?
challenges.explanation: The bug is using % 3 instead of % 2. To check if a number is even or odd, you always divide by 2. turnNumber % 3 gives the remainder after dividing by 3 that's a completely different thing. Fix: change % 3 to % 2.
